kinesisSSEDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have server-side encryption disabled (EncryptionType=NONE). Data at rest is stored in plaintext on the stream, blocking regulated workloads and audit of key usage. This is Security Hub control Kinesis.1.
kinesisSSEDisabled.shortDesc: Enable server-side encryption on every Kinesis stream

kinesisSSEDefaultKey.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams are encrypted with the default AWS-managed key (alias/aws/kinesis) rather than a customer-managed KMS key (CMK). AWS-managed keys are functional for many workloads but do not permit key policy customisation, cross-account access, or independent rotation control. Organisations with CMK mandates should upgrade.
kinesisSSEDefaultKey.shortDesc: Prefer a customer-managed KMS key over alias/aws/kinesis

kinesisKMSKeyRotationDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams use a customer-managed KMS key (CMK) with automatic key rotation disabled. Compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, NIST SP 800-57) recommend annual rotation of symmetric encryption keys. AWS-managed keys and streams using alias/aws/kinesis are excluded from this check.
kinesisKMSKeyRotationDisabled.shortDesc: Enable automatic key rotation on the customer-managed CMK

kinesisRetentionPeriodMinimum.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have a retention period below the recommended 168 hours (7 days). Short retention increases the risk of data loss during downstream consumer outages, replay scenarios, or investigations. Minimum is 24 hours; maximum is 8760 hours (365 days). Security Hub Kinesis.3 recommends >= 168 hours.
kinesisRetentionPeriodMinimum.shortDesc: Increase RetentionPeriodHours to at least 168 (7 days)

kinesisNoTags.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have no user-defined tags (system tags with the aws: prefix are excluded). Tags are required for cost allocation, resource ownership tracking, and automated governance policies. This is Security Hub control Kinesis.2.
kinesisNoTags.shortDesc: Add owner, environment, and cost-centre tags to every stream

kinesisEnhancedMonitoringDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have enhanced (shard-level) monitoring disabled entirely. Without shard-level CloudWatch metrics you cannot identify hot shards, per-shard throttling, or uneven partition-key distribution — the most common troubleshooting scenarios for Kinesis.

kinesisStreamNotActive.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams are not in ACTIVE state (currently CREATING, UPDATING, or DELETING). CREATING/UPDATING are normally transient — a stream stuck in either state for more than ~10 minutes indicates a service issue. DELETING may indicate failed cleanup that leaves an orphaned resource.
kinesisStreamNotActive.shortDesc: Investigate streams stuck in non-ACTIVE states

kinesisProvisionedModeNoAutoScaling.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams use PROVISIONED capacity mode. Provisioned mode requires manual shard management or an external Application Auto Scaling configuration; without either, spikes in producer traffic cause WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded errors. This check is advisory — provisioned mode is optimal for predictable workloads with tight cost control.
kinesisProvisionedModeNoAutoScaling.shortDesc: Confirm auto-scaling is configured or workload is predictable

kinesisShardUtilizationHigh.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams are in an account whose open-shard count has exceeded 80% of the account-wide service quota (from DescribeLimits, default 200). New stream creation or shard splits will fail once the limit is hit. Request a quota increase before reaching capacity.
kinesisShardUtilizationHigh.shortDesc: Request an open-shard quota increase or reduce shard count

kinesisNoConsumers.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have zero enhanced fan-out (EFO) consumers registered and have been ACTIVE for more than 7 days. This is advisory: KCL consumers, Lambda triggers, and Firehose sources do NOT register as EFO consumers and therefore this check cannot definitively prove a stream is unused. Verify the stream is actually being read before deleting.
kinesisNoConsumers.shortDesc: Verify the stream is actively consumed or delete it

kinesisOnDemandPredictableWorkload.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have been in ON_DEMAND mode for more than 30 days. On-demand scales automatically but costs ~20% more than a right-sized provisioned stream for predictable throughput. This is purely informational — many organisations prefer on-demand for operational simplicity.
kinesisOnDemandPredictableWorkload.shortDesc: Review whether provisioned mode is more cost-effective

kinesisEnhancedMonitoringPartial.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have partial shard-level monitoring — some metrics are enabled but at least one of the critical operational metrics (IteratorAgeMilliseconds, WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded, ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded) is missing. Without these, throttling and consumer-lag incidents cannot be diagnosed per shard.
kinesisEnhancedMonitoringPartial.shortDesc: Enable ALL shard-level metrics or at least the three critical ones

kinesisConsumerStuck.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have enhanced fan-out consumer(s) stuck in CREATING or DELETING state for more than 10 minutes. Consumer state transitions should complete within seconds — a stuck consumer typically indicates missing IAM permissions or an underlying service issue.
kinesisConsumerStuck.shortDesc: Investigate and force-delete stuck EFO consumers

kinesisNoCloudWatchAlarms.^description: {$COUNT} Kinesis Data Streams have no CloudWatch alarms configured on any of the critical operational metrics (GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds, WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded, ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded). Without alarms, throttling and consumer-lag incidents go undetected until downstream systems fail.
kinesisNoCloudWatchAlarms.shortDesc: Configure alarms on IteratorAge and throughput-exceeded metrics
